critique.summary: The article discusses ICML 2026's policies on the use of large language models (LLMs) in peer reviews, detailing that about 2% of submissions were desk rejected due to reviewers violating the no-LLM-use policy. It explains the detection method involving watermarking submission PDFs and the enforcement actions taken against reviewers who breached the agreed-upon rules.
mainPoints: ICML 2026 implemented two policies regarding LLM use in reviewing: a conservative no-LLM policy (Policy A) and a permissive policy allowing limited LLM use (Policy B).
Approximately 2% of submissions (497 papers) were desk rejected because their reviewers, assigned to the no-LLM policy, used LLMs in their reviews.
Detection involved watermarking submission PDFs with hidden instructions to identify LLM-generated content, followed by manual verification to avoid false positives.
Reviewers who violated the no-LLM policy had their reviews removed, and repeat offenders were removed from the reviewer pool.
The approach acknowledges limitations, including potential circumvention of watermarking and incomplete detection coverage.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information about ICML's evolving policies on AI use in peer review and the challenges posed by LLMs. The described watermarking technique and enforcement measures are plausible and consistent with recent research on AI-generated text detection. However, since the article is from a conference blog and does not cite external sources or independent verification, some details, such as exact percentages and detection efficacy, rely on ICML's internal reporting. The overall narrative is credible but would benefit from corroboration by independent sources or official ICML communications.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential ethical implications or privacy concerns related to watermarking PDFs and monitoring reviewer behavior.
It does not address how the policies might impact reviewer workload or the quality of reviews beyond LLM use.
The effectiveness of watermarking as a detection method may decrease over time as users find ways to circumvent it.
The article does not explore the broader debate within the research community about the appropriate role of AI tools in peer review.
There is no mention of the perspectives or responses from reviewers who were penalized or from authors affected by desk rejections.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around ICML's policy enforcement against the use of LLMs in reviews, highlighting a novel detection method using hidden prompt injections in PDFs. Commenters debate the fairness, effectiveness, and broader implications of this approach, with some noting potential underreporting and ethical concerns.

comments.author: bonoboTP
comments.text:
To be clear, as the article says, these authors were offered a choice and agreed to be on the "no LLMs allowed" policy.

And detection was not done with some snake oil "AI detector" but by invisible prompt injection in the paper pdf, instructing LLMs to put TWO long phrases into the review. They then detected LLM use through checking if both phrases appear in the review.

This did not detect grammar checks and touchups of an independently written review. The phrases would only get included if the reviewer fed the pdf to the LLM in clear violation to their chosen policy.

> After a selection process, in which reviewers got to choose which policy they would like to operate under, they were assigned to either Policy A or Policy B. In the end, based on author demands and reviewer signups, the only reviewers who were assigned to Policy A (no LLMs) were those who explicitly selected “Policy A” or “I am okay with either [Policy] A or B.” To be clear, no reviewer who strongly preferred Policy B was assigned to Policy A.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately explains the detection mechanism and clarifies the policy choice process, providing a clear and factual overview.

comments.author: mijoharas
comments.text:
One thing to note.

They were quite conservative in their approach, so the only things that were rejected were from people who had agreed not to use an LLM and almost definitely did use an LLM (since they fed hidden watermarked instructions to the llm's).

This means the true number of people that used LLM's in their review (even in group A that had agreed not to) is likely higher.

Also worth noting, 10% of these authors used them in more than half of their reviews.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully points out that the detection method was conservative, implying underestimation of LLM use, which aligns with the limitations of detection.

comments.author: mika-el
comments.text:
The irony here is that the detection method is literally prompt injection — the same technique that's a security vulnerability everywhere else. ICML embedded hidden instructions in PDFs that manipulate LLM output. In a different context that's an attack, here it's enforcement.

From my perspective this says something important about where we are with LLMs. The fact that you can reliably manipulate model output by hiding instructions in the input means the model has no real separation between data and commands. That's the fundamental problem whether you're catching lazy reviewers or defending against actual attacks.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced perspective on the detection method's dual nature as both a vulnerability and enforcement tool, highlighting fundamental LLM issues.

comments.author: sampo
comments.text:
Took me a while understand. So, the same person has both submitted their research article to the conference, and also acted as a reviewer for articles submitted by other people.

And if they in their review work have agreed to a "no LLM use" policy, but got exposed using LLMs anyway, then their submitted research article is desk rejected. Theoretically, someone could have submitted a stellar research article, but because they didn't follow agreed policy when reviewing other people's work, then also their research contribution is not welcome.

(At first I understood that innocent author's articles would have been rejected just because they happened to go to a bad reviewer. But this is not the case.)
comments.analysis: This comment correctly clarifies the policy consequence linking reviewer misconduct to desk rejection of their own submissions, addressing a common misunderstanding.

comments.author: hodgehog11
comments.text:
I'm amazed that such a simple method of detection worked so flawlessly for so many people. This would not work for those who merely used LLMs to help pinpoint strengths and weaknesses in the paper; there are separate techniques to judge that. Instead, it only detects those who quite literally copied and pasted the LLM output as a review.

It's incredible how so many people thought it was fair that their paper should be assessed by human reviewers alone, and yet would not extend the same courtesy to others.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately differentiates between outright LLM output copying and more subtle assistance, reflecting the detection method's scope.

comments.author: merelysounds
comments.text:
Related discussion elsewhere and from a different point of view:

> ICML: every paper in my review batch contains prompt-injection text embedded in the PDF

source: https://old.reddit.com/r/MachineLearning/comments/1r3oekq/d_...

There are recent comments there as well:

> Desk Reject Comments: The paper is desk rejected, because the reciprocal reviewer nominated for this paper ([OpenReview ID redacted]) has violated the LLM reviewing policy. The reviewer was required to follow Policy A (no LLMs), but we have found a strong evidence that LLM was used in the preparation of at least one of their reviews. This is a breach of peer-review ethics and grounds for desk rejection. (...)

source: https://old.reddit.com/r/MachineLearning/comments/1r3oekq/d_...
comments.analysis: This comment provides corroborating external evidence supporting the enforcement actions and ethical framing of the policy.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text: I keep spotting clear LLM 'tells' in text where I know the people on the other side believe they're 'getting away with it'. It is incredible at what levels of commerce people do this, and how they're prepared to risk their reputation by saving a few characters typed. It makes me wonder what they think they are getting paid for.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a personal observation about LLM usage and its risks, which is plausible but anecdotal.

comments.author: quinndupont
comments.text:
How is nobody considering the broader political economy of scholarly publications and reviews? These are UNPAID reviews! Sure, maybe ICML isn’t Elsevier, but they are cousins to the socially parasitic and exploitative companies, at the very least.

Hiding behind a false “choice” to not use AI or basically not use AI isn’t an appropriate proposal. This is crooked and shameful. We should boycott ICML except we can’t because they are already the gatekeepers!
comments.analysis: This comment raises valid socio-economic concerns about academic publishing and review labor, though it is more opinionated than factual.

comments.author: mvrckhckr
comments.text: It’s ironic. I also doubt the validity of the AI writing detection.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses skepticism about detection validity without supporting evidence, making it less reliable.

comments.author: causalityltd
comments.text:
The declaration of no-LLM was done for social prestige or maybe self-deception of self-sufficiency like "I don't need LLM". And when it was time to do the actual work, the dependency kicked in like drugs. 
A lesson for all of us with LLMs in our workflow.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses human behavior around LLM use, though it is speculative about motivations.
